title: Parmesan and herb scones
subtitle: Savoury cheese scones with rosemary and chives
excerpt: Savoury scones made with parmesan and fresh herbs: rosemary in the dough and chives stirred through, with extra parmesan on top that blisters and browns as they bake. They are best eaten warm, split and buttered, alongside soup or with a ploughman's board. The technique is the same as a classic scone (cold fat, minimal working, high heat) but the cheese in the dough keeps them moister than plain scones for longer.
sourceNotes: Savoury scones with cheese belong to the British scone tradition, which dates to at least the early nineteenth century. Cheese scones appear in British women's magazines and household cookbooks from the early twentieth century, particularly in wartime domestic economy manuals. The addition of parmesan in place of cheddar and fresh herbs is associated with the Italian influence on British cooking from the 1980s. Delia Smith's cheese scone recipe in 'Complete Cookery Course' (1982) helped standardise the home version.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat at 160 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. Reheat briefly in the oven for 5 minutes to restore the crust.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; high heat gives maximum rise and a golden crust

content.text: The rules for cheese scones are the same as for plain ones: cold butter, minimum handling, and a hot oven. Overworked scone dough develops gluten and produces a tight, tough crumb. The parmesan goes in the dough and on top; the top portion melts and caramelises. The rosemary must be fresh; dried rosemary has a woody texture that does not soften in 15 minutes of baking.

items.prepNote: finely grated, plus 20 g extra for the tops

content.text: Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray. Sift 300 g flour, 2 tsp baking powder, and 0.5 tsp salt into a large bowl. Rub in 80 g cold cubed butter until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs (a few larger pieces are fine. Stir in 80 g grated parmesan, 1 tsp chopped rosemary, 2 tbsp chives, and 0.5 tsp black pepper. Beat together 150 ml cold milk and 1 egg. Pour into the flour mixture and mix briefly with a fork until just combined) do not overwork. Turn onto a floured surface. Pat out gently to about 3 cm thickness. Cut into 8 rounds with a 7 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the lined tray. Brush tops with milk and scatter the extra 20 g parmesan over. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes until risen and golden. Best eaten warm.

items.solution: The cutter was twisted rather than pressed straight down, sealing the edges and preventing the rise. Always press and lift — no twisting. Also check the baking powder is in date.
The dough was overworked after the liquid was added. Mix just until the flour is incorporated — a shaggy, rough dough that just holds together is correct.

items.problem: Parmesan on top burned before the scones were cooked through
items.solution: Tent loosely with foil after 8 minutes if the tops are colouring too quickly. The scones need the full 13 to 15 minutes to cook through.
